title: IFNL4 / peginterferon alfa-2a
summary: Patients with the rs368234815 GG genotype and chronic hepatitis C infection may have decreased response (including sustained virological response (svr)) to pegIFN-alpha/ribavirin as compared to people with with TT/TT genotype. However, conflicting evidence has been reported. Please note: this SNP has been found to be in high LD with rs12979860 and rs8099917, both of which are also associated with response to pegIFN-alpha/ribavirin. Other genetic and clinical factors may also influence the response to pegIFN-alpha/ribavirin-based therapy.
riskSummary: IFNL4 variant associated with efficacy for peginterferon alfa-2a
detail: Patients with the rs368234815 GG genotype and chronic hepatitis C infection may have decreased response (including sustained virological response (svr)) to pegIFN-alpha/ribavirin as compared to people with with TT/TT genotype. However, conflicting evidence has been reported. Please note: this SNP has been found to be in high LD with rs12979860 and rs8099917, both of which are also associated with response to pegIFN-alpha/ribavirin. whyItMatters: PharmGKB curates pharmacogenomic evidence from the literature, with level 1A–2B annotations backed by expert review and published clinical studies.
notes: PharmGKB annotations reflect published literature and should not replace clinical pharmacogenomic testing.

title: VKORC1 / warfarin

title: 2 - 4 fold increased odds of developing V617F-positive MPN
summary: 2 - 4 fold increased odds of developing V617F-positive MPN
detail: A Promethease user who has this rs12340895(G;G) genotype has reported that they have mastocytosis, a myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN). But don't panic! Mastocytosis is listed as a rare disease by the Office of Rare Diseases (ORD) of the National Institutes of Health, which means mastocytosis affects less than 200,000 people in the entire US. To put it another way, 5 to 10 new cases are reported annually per million people. Since this rs12340895(G;G) genotype is carried by many people (e.g. ~10% of all Caucasians, or, over 30 million Americans), it's clear the overwhelming majority do not develop mastocytosis.
whyItMatters: SNPedia has a genotype-specific page for this result, which can add consumer-facing context alongside primary evidence sources.

title: Asian Flusher; increased risk of esophageal cancer; East Asian ancestry; Disulfiram not effec...
summary: Asian Flusher; increased risk of esophageal cancer; East Asian ancestry; Disulfiram not effective for alcoholism.
detail: People with this genotype will experience the asian flush, a redness in the face when drinking alcohol. Unable to break down acetaldehyde, a toxic byproduct of alcohol and cigarette smoke. As a result, you are likely to have greatly increased odds of esophageal cancer, especially if you drink or smoke. It also causes worse hangovers. On the bright side, that makes you much less likely to become an alcoholic. If you become an alcoholic anyway, disulfiram (Antabuse) will not be an effective treatment.
whyItMatters: SNPedia has a genotype-specific page for this result, which can add consumer-facing context alongside primary evidence sources.

title: Asian Flush; worse hangovers; increased risk of esophageal cancer; East Asian ancestry; Disul...
summary: Asian Flush; worse hangovers; increased risk of esophageal cancer; East Asian ancestry; Disulfiram probably not effective for alcoholism.
detail: People with this genotype will experience the asian flush, a redness in the face when drinking alcohol. They can only break down acetaldehyde at half the normal speed, a toxic byproduct of alcohol and cigarette smoke. As a result, you are likely to have increased odds of esophageal cancer, especially if you drink or smoke. It also causes worse hangovers. On the bright side, that makes you less likely to become an alcoholic. If you become an alcoholic anyway, disulfiram (Antabuse) probably won't be an effective treatment.
whyItMatters: SNPedia has a genotype-specific page for this result, which can add consumer-facing context alongside primary evidence sources.
conditions: Disulfiram probably not effective for alcoholism.

People with this genotype will experience the asian flush, a redness in the face when drinking alcohol. They can only break down acetaldehyde at half the normal speed, a toxic byproduct of alcohol and cigarette smoke. As a result, you are likely to have increased odds of esophageal cancer, especially if you drink or smoke. It also causes worse hangovers. On the bright side, that makes you less likely to become an alcoholic. If you become an alcoholic anyway, disulfiram (Antabuse) probably won't be an effective treatment.

title: Alcohol Flush: Normal, doesn't flush. Normal hangovers. Normal risk of Alcoholism. Normal ris...
summary: Alcohol Flush: Normal, doesn't flush. Normal hangovers. Normal risk of Alcoholism. Normal risk of Esophageal Cancer. Disulfiram is effective for alcoholism.
detail: Two working copies of Aldehyde Dehydrogenase (ALDH2) mean you won't turn bright red when you drink alcohol, unlike most Asians. After your body turns alcohol into the dangerous acetaldehyde, this gene quickly turns the acetaldehyde into vinegar. It also stops the acetaldehyde in cigarettes from causing Esophageal Cancer. It reduces hangovers compared to Asians, but less hangovers and flushing obviously increases the risk of alcoholism. Alcoholism can be treated effectively with the drug Disulfiram (Antabuse) which would stop this gene from working and cause the Asian flush in non-Asians. PMC2659709 [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2659709/]"AALDH2-deficient individuals are at much higher risk of esophageal cancer (specifically squamous cell carcinoma) from alcohol consumption than individuals with fully active ALDH2. This is particularly unfortunate as esophageal cancer is one of the deadliest cancers worldwide, with five-year survival rates of 15.6% in the United States, 12.3% in Europe, and 31.6% in Japan"
whyItMatters: SNPedia has a genotype-specific page for this result, which can add consumer-facing context alongside primary evidence sources.
conditions: Alcohol Flush: Normal, doesn't flush. Normal hangovers. Normal risk of Alcoholism. Normal risk of Esophageal Cancer. Disulfiram is effective for alcoholism.

Two working copies of Aldehyde Dehydrogenase (ALDH2) mean you won't turn bright red when you drink alcohol, unlike most Asians. After your body turns alcohol into the dangerous acetaldehyde, this gene quickly turns the acetaldehyde into vinegar. It also stops the acetaldehyde in cigarettes from causing Esophageal Cancer. It reduces hangovers compared to Asians, but less hangovers and flushing obviously increases the risk of alcoholism. Alcoholism can be treated effectively with the drug Disulfiram (Antabuse) which would stop this gene from working and cause the Asian flush in non-Asians. PMC2659709 [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2659709/]"AALDH2-deficient individuals are at much higher risk of esophageal cancer (specifically squamous cell carcinoma) from alcohol consumption than individuals with fully active ALDH2. This is particularly unfortunate as esophageal cancer is one of the deadliest cancers worldwide, with five-year survival rates of 15.6% in the United States, 12.3% in Europe, and 31.6% in Japan"
